content:
Batch Sending Crypto Across 3 Chains? Here’s How We Solved It Without Losing Our Minds
A few months ago, we were building a reward distribution system for a Web3 project. Every week, we had to send small amounts of ETH, SOL, and sometimes even BTC to hundreds of wallets. Sounds simple, right?
It wasn’t.
We quickly realized that handling batch transactions manually is a nightmare. You have to juggle different libraries for each chain (ethers.js for Ethereum, @solana/web3.js for Solana, and bitcoinjs-lib for Bitcoin). Each of them has its own way of handling nonces, gas fees, and transaction signing.
We were spending more time debugging nonce errors than actually building features. And don’t even get me started on gas optimization — sending transactions one by one was burning through our budget like crazy.
That’s why we ended up building @web3-helpers/core.
It’s a lightweight Node.js library that wraps all three chains into a single, consistent API. No more context switching. No more “nonce too low” errors at 2 AM. Just a few lines of code, and you’re done.

What It Actually Does
The core idea is pretty simple: you give it a list of wallet addresses and an amount, and it handles the rest.
Here’s a real example of how we use it to send ETH to multiple recipients:
javascript
const { sendBatch } = require('@web3-helpers/core');const recipients = [
'0x1234567890123456789012345678901234567890',
'0xabcdefabcdefabcdefabcdefabcdefabcdefabcd',
];const tx = await sendBatch(recipients, '0.01');
console.log('Batch TX hash:', tx.hash);That’s it. Under the hood, it manages nonces, calculates gas fees, and broadcasts the transaction. The same pattern works for Solana and Bitcoin too (with slightly different methods).
